relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: After Ehud dies, the cycle turns again.
Israel falls under Jabin of Hazor and Sisera's command.
Nine hundred iron chariots and twenty years of force drive Israel to cry out.
Deborah stands in the text as woman, prophetess, and judge.
Under her palm, Israel comes to receive judgment.
She summons Barak and places YHWH's command before him.
Sisera's chariots will be drawn to Kishon and given into Barak's hand.
Barak will not go unless Deborah goes with him.
Deborah agrees, but says the honor will pass through a woman's hand.
Naphtali and Zebulun gather, and Deborah goes up with Barak.
Heber the Kenite's separated tent creates another political doorway.
Sisera hears that Barak has gone to Tabor.
Iron chariots gather toward Kishon.
Deborah names the day and sends Barak down.
The chariot commander panics and becomes a man fleeing on foot.
The army of chariots collapses until no one remains.
Sisera runs toward Jael's tent, trusting political peace with Heber's house.
Jael opens the tent as refuge and covers him.
He asks for water and receives milk.
He asks her to guard a lie at the tent entrance.
Jael takes tent peg and hammer and kills the sleeping oppressor.
She brings Barak to the dead man he sought.
Jabin's power is subdued that day.
Israel's hand presses harder until Jabin is cut off.

contemplative_note: Judges 4 centers women where the battle turns: Deborah speaks the command, accompanies the fearful leader, and Jael ends the oppressor's flight with tools of the tent. Their agency is unmistakable; the violence remains morally dangerous and must not be made cute.
